Adult Echo / Aorta Reference

Adult Cardiac Size Reference

Adult cardiac measurements are usually interpreted using absolute dimensions, indexed values, and guideline-based thresholds rather than pediatric Z-score models. This tool summarizes common adult echocardiographic and aortic reference values and provides simple indexed calculations for clinical support.

For clinical reference only. Measurements must be interpreted with image quality, modality, diagnosis, symptoms, and institutional protocols.
Aorta Chambers Valve severity Calculators References

Patient size

No patient history is stored. Enter BSA directly or calculate it from height and weight.

Adult aortic size reference

Enter diameters in mm. Results show cm, cm/m², cm/m, and cross-sectional area divided by height.

Adult chamber reference

Phase 2 will add sex-specific LV, LA, RV, and RA reference checks from adult chamber quantification guidance. For now, use the indexed-value calculator below for LV mass index, LA volume index, stroke volume index, and AVA index.

Valve severity quick reference

Phase 3 will add AS, AR, MS, MR, and TR severity quick checks using guideline-based reference language. Discordant parameters should be interpreted with flow state, blood pressure, rhythm, ventricular function, and the full echo report.

LVEF from EDV / ESV

LVEF: —

Continuity equation AVA

AVA: — · AVA index: —

Indexed value

Value / BSA: —

Aortic area / height

Area / height: —

Methodology & limitations expand

Adult cardiac measurements are interpreted using a combination of absolute dimensions, indexed values, modality-specific reference ranges, and guideline-based thresholds. Unlike pediatric echocardiography, adult cardiac structure assessment generally does not rely on universal Z-score models. Results may differ depending on imaging modality, measurement convention, sex, body size, age, diagnosis, and institutional protocol.

  • Echo, CT, CMR, and intraoperative TEE may not be interchangeable.
  • Aortic measurements should specify anatomical level and leading-edge versus inner-edge convention when relevant.
  • Valve severity should be interpreted using an integrated approach, not a single parameter.
  • Guideline thresholds are not a substitute for clinician judgment.
  • Documented formulas include Mosteller, DuBois, and Haycock BSA; LVEF = (EDV − ESV) / EDV × 100; AVA = LVOT area × LVOT VTI / AV VTI; and aortic area / height = π × (diameterCm / 2)² / heightM.

FAQ

Do adults use cardiac Z-scores?

Adult cardiac measurements are usually interpreted using absolute dimensions, indexed values, and guideline thresholds. Pediatric Z-score models should not be directly applied to adults.

Why index adult cardiac measurements to BSA?

Body size affects cardiac chamber and aortic dimensions. Indexing to BSA or height can help contextualize measurements, especially in very small or very large patients.

What is cross-sectional aortic area divided by height?

It is a body-size-adjusted aortic risk metric calculated from aortic diameter and patient height. It is often used as an adjunct to absolute diameter in aortic disease assessment.

Can this tool decide whether a patient needs surgery?

No. It provides reference values and guideline-based caution flags. Final decisions require diagnosis, symptoms, imaging quality, serial change, operative risk, and clinician judgment.

Are echo, CT, and CMR measurements interchangeable?

No. Measurement conventions and imaging planes differ by modality. Use the same modality and measurement convention for serial follow-up whenever possible.

Why do valve severity parameters sometimes disagree?

Valve severity can be affected by flow state, blood pressure, rhythm, LV/RV function, loading conditions, and measurement error. Discordant findings require integrated interpretation.

Selected references

Reference equations and guideline thresholds are implemented for educational and clinical support use. Always confirm measurement technique, anatomical definition, and selected reference before applying results clinically.

Lang RM, Badano LP, Mor-Avi V, et al. Recommendations for Cardiac Chamber Quantification by Echocardiography in Adults. J Am Soc Echocardiogr. 2015.

PubMedDOI

Devereux RB, et al. Normal limits in relation to age, body size and gender of two-dimensional echocardiographic aortic root dimensions. Am J Cardiol. 2012.

PubMedDOI

Patel HN, et al. Normal values of aortic root size according to age, sex, and race: results of the WASE study. J Am Soc Echocardiogr. 2022.

PubMedGuideline

Isselbacher EM, et al. 2022 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Aortic Disease.

PubMedDOIGuideline

Zoghbi WA, et al. Recommendations for Noninvasive Evaluation of Native Valvular Regurgitation. J Am Soc Echocardiogr. 2017.

PubMedDOI

Baumgartner H, et al. Recommendations on the Echocardiographic Assessment of Aortic Valve Stenosis: A Focused Update. J Am Soc Echocardiogr / Eur Heart J Cardiovasc Imaging. 2017.

PubMedGuideline
